name: Seminoe Reservoir
short_description: BLM rockhounding site at high-elevation Seminoe Reservoir featuring agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens in sagebrush flats and desert wash terrain. Free public collecting with standard BLM limits.
description:
Seminoe Reservoir sits in the high desert of Carbon County at 6370 feet elevation, where BLM public land surrounds the reservoir and downstream areas. This location offers excellent opportunities for collectors seeking quality agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens in relatively accessible terrain. The flat sagebrush landscape makes searching easier than many Rocky Mountain sites, though the elevation and exposure to wind require proper preparation. Rockhounds typically focus efforts on exposed wash banks, desert pavement areas, and small outcrops where silica-rich materials weather out naturally. The reservoir area is less crowded than many popular Wyoming rockhounding destinations, making it ideal for those seeking a quieter collecting experience without the permit hassles or fees associated with commercial dig sites.

The region's geological history traces to Miocene-era volcanic activity and subsequent basin-and-range faulting that exposed and fractured silica-bearing rock formations. These processes created ideal conditions for agate and chalcedony development in fractures and cavities. Modern erosion continues to expose new specimens across the desert surface, particularly in gullies and washes where water flow concentrates heavier minerals and loosens specimens from surrounding host rock. Elevation and latitude combine to create a semi-arid climate with distinct seasons, making timing and weather preparation critical factors for successful trips.

Visitors should expect wide-open sagebrush country with minimal shade and exposure to wind and sun. The flat terrain allows you to cover ground efficiently, but the vastness can be disorienting. Cell service is spotty, so download offline maps and carry navigation tools. Water sources are limited, making it essential to bring your own supply. The area experiences significant temperature swings between seasons, ranging from freezing winters to hot, dry summers.
history: Seminoe Reservoir area sits within the Medicine Bow Basin, where Miocene-era volcanic activity deposited silica-rich mineral solutions that crystallized into agate and chalcedony veins and nodules. The exposed formations and modern erosional processes continue to release collectible specimens at the surface, making this a naturally productive area for recreational rockhounding.
tips: Focus your search on desert washes and small drainage channels where water flow concentrates heavier specimens and erosion naturally exposes new material. Look for specimens with translucent banding and varied coloring (reds, whites, grays, browns) in the agate and jasper finds. Bring a 5-gallon bucket or collection bag to stay organized as you work across large areas. Use a small hand pick or rock hammer to test specimens in situ before collecting, as many appear more promising in the ground than in your hand.
terrain_notes: Flat to gently rolling sagebrush desert at 6370 feet elevation. Well-drained sandy-gravelly soil with scattered volcanic rocks. Minimal vegetation except low sage. Walking distance from parking areas typically ranges from 0.5 to 2 miles depending on your interest. Footing is relatively stable on desert pavement and packed soil, though loose rocks and cobbles require careful stepping in some areas. No shade available.
written_directions: From Rawlins (approximately 25 miles south), take WY-287 north toward Saratoga. Continue approximately 35 miles north past Saratoga on WY-130 toward Sinclair. Turn northeast on Seminoe Road (County Road 403) and follow it for approximately 18 miles to reach the Seminoe Reservoir area and BLM public lands. Parking pullouts occur at multiple locations along the reservoir perimeter and upstream along the North Platte River drainage. Request specific access information at the BLM office in Rawlins before heading out.
road_conditions: Seminoe Road transitions from paved highway to gravel and dirt approximately 20 miles before the reservoir. Surface is generally passable in passenger vehicles during dry season, but becomes rough and muddy after precipitation. High-clearance or all-wheel drive recommended during spring runoff or wet conditions.
parking_notes: Multiple unmarked pullouts and informal parking areas along Seminoe Road and at reservoir access points. No developed parking lot or facilities. Space available for 2-4 vehicles at most pullouts. Camp-style parking allowed on BLM land per standard regulations.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Pack out all trash. Respect water resource restrictions around the reservoir.
rules:
Personal use only

No motorized excavation or heavy equipment
Pack out all trash
Respect water resource areas and private land boundaries
nearest_services: Rawlins (approximately 60 miles south) has gas, food, lodging, and full services. Saratoga (approximately 40 miles south) offers limited supplies and services.
accessibility_notes: Flat terrain accessible to most hikers and mobility levels. Parking pullouts are at ground level with no developed trails. Walking across sagebrush is manageable, though uneven rocky surfaces require sturdy footwear. No wheelchair accessibility at parking areas. Distance from vehicle to productive collecting grounds is flexible - you can work close to parking or hike farther.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Seminoe Reservoir in Wyoming?
faq.answer: Seminoe Reservoir is known for quality agate, jasper, chalcedony, and quartz specimens. Collectors typically find translucent to opaque pieces with attractive banding and coloring in reds, whites, grays, and browns. Most specimens range from thumbnail size to fist-sized, though larger pieces are occasionally discovered in washes.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Seminoe Reservoir?
faq.answer: No permit is required to rockhound on BLM land at Seminoe Reservoir. This is public land open to free recreational collecting under FLPMA regulations, with a 25-pound daily limit per person. You can collect any time year-round without advance notification or permits.

faq.question: What is the best time to visit Seminoe Reservoir for rockhounding?
faq.answer: June through September offers the best rockhounding conditions, with stable weather, passable roads, and manageable temperatures for outdoor collecting. Winter is possible but roads become difficult, temperatures drop below freezing, and snow may limit access. Spring and early summer water runoff can improve specimen exposure but may close some areas.

faq.question: How far is Seminoe Reservoir from major towns, and what supplies should I bring?
faq.answer: Seminoe Reservoir is approximately 60 miles from Rawlins and 40 miles from Saratoga. Plan for a remote location - bring plenty of water (at least 2 gallons per person), sun protection, a hat, and warm layers. Cell service is spotty, so download offline maps. Gas and food are available in Rawlins and Saratoga.

faq.question: Is Seminoe Reservoir rockhounding suitable for beginners and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, Seminoe Reservoir is excellent for beginners and families. The flat, open terrain makes it easy to search and cover ground efficiently. Children ages 6 and up can participate. There are no technical skills required, no fees, and no permits to worry about. Bring plenty of water, sun protection, and supervise young children in the remote setting.

faq.question: What tools and gear do I need to rockhound at Seminoe Reservoir?
faq.answer: Bring a small hand pick or rock hammer for testing specimens, a collection bag or 5-gallon bucket, sturdy hiking boots, sunscreen, hat, and at least 2 gallons of water. A loupe or magnifying glass helps identify quality specimens. Since roads can be rough, a high-clearance or all-wheel-drive vehicle is recommended, especially in wet conditions or shoulder seasons.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding at Seminoe Reservoir for agate, jasper, and chalcedony. No permit required, 25 lb/day limit. Remote high-desert site near Rawlins.
